hallucinations.description: The paper title and abstract claim the comparison is between 'RL-Aligned and DPO-Aligned' models, but CodeLlama-7B-Instruct is NOT a DPO-aligned model. It is a general instruction-tuned model. The paper itself acknowledges this in Section 3.1 ('It is a general instruction-following model, not specifically trained with code-focused DPO') and Section 6.2, yet the title, abstract, and throughout the paper it is repeatedly framed as an RL vs DPO comparison. The code also labels CodeLlama-Instruct as 'dpo' throughout (e.g., dpo_model_id, samples_dpo.jsonl, dpo_results). This is a fundamental mischaracterization of the methodology.
hallucinations.evidence: Title: 'Error Type Distributions Differ Between RL-Aligned and DPO-Aligned Code Generation Models'; Config: 'dpo_model_id: str = "codellama/CodeLlama-7b-Instruct-hf"'; Paper Section 3.1: 'It is a general instruction-following model, not specifically trained with code-focused DPO.'

hallucinations.description: The paper cites 'Wang et al. (2025). Understanding Code Generation Failures: A Systematic Analysis of LLM-Generated Code Errors. ICSE.' as an 'ICSE 2025 study' providing a three-tier error classification. This paper cannot be verified to exist. The citation appears fabricated - no such paper by these authors with this title can be found in ICSE 2025 proceedings.
hallucinations.evidence: Section 2.3: 'The ICSE 2025 study by Wang et al. (2025) provides a three-tier classification based on execution semantics.' References: 'Wang, Y., Chen, B., Zhang, J., & Liu, X. (2025). Understanding Code Generation Failures: A Systematic Analysis of LLM-Generated Code Errors. ICSE.'

hallucinations.description: The paper cites 'Zhang et al. (2024). LlmFix: A Large Language Model-Based Code Repair Framework with Fine-Grained Error Taxonomy. arXiv:2409.00676.' This arXiv paper ID does not correspond to a paper called 'LlmFix' with a 19-category error taxonomy. The paper and its 19-cause taxonomy appear to be fabricated.
hallucinations.evidence: Section 2.3: 'The LlmFix framework (Zhang et al., 2024) extends this to 19 distinct error causes.' References: 'Zhang, W., Li, M., Wang, T., & Chen, H. (2024). LlmFix: A Large Language Model-Based Code Repair Framework with Fine-Grained Error Taxonomy. arXiv:2409.00676.'

hallucinations.description: The paper claims to use the 'LlmFix 19-category taxonomy' from Zhang et al. (2024), but examining the code reveals this taxonomy was entirely constructed by the authors in config.py. The 19 categories (indentation_error, syntax_error, missing_import, name_error, type_error, attribute_error, index_error, key_error, value_error, zero_division, recursion_error, timeout, memory_error, wrong_output, partial_output, missing_output, wrong_type, off_by_one, boundary_error) are defined in the code without any evidence they come from an external published framework.

The paper references Liu et al. (2024) with the citation 'Is Your Code Generated by ChatGPT Really Correct? Rigorous Evaluation of Large Language Models for Code Generation. NeurIPS 36.' This paper is actually by Liu et al. (2024) published at NeurIPS 2023 (volume 36), but the paper uses it in the context of 'Subsequent work has explored variations including... end-to-end execution feedback (Liu et al., 2024)' which conflates two different Liu et al. 2024 references - one about EvalPlus and one about RLEF.
hallucinations.evidence: References list two Liu et al. 2024 entries: 'Liu, J., Xia, C. S., Wang, Y., & Zhang, L. (2024). Is Your Code Generated by ChatGPT Really Correct?...' and 'Liu, X., Tian, R., Jia, Z., et al. (2024). RLEF: Grounding Code LLMs in Execution Feedback...'

hallucinations.description: The paper reports CodeRL achieving a 56.5% pass rate on HumanEval+ and MBPP+ combined, which is implausibly high for a 770M parameter CodeT5-large model. The original CodeRL paper (Le et al., 2022) reports much lower pass rates. The CodeT5-large-ntp-py model is a next-token prediction model, not the full CodeRL model with RL training. The paper misrepresents this base model as 'CodeRL (RL-aligned)' when it may not have received the RL training described in the CodeRL paper.
hallucinations.evidence: Section 5.1: 'CodeRL: Pass Rate 56.5%'; Config: 'rl_model_id: str = "Salesforce/codet5-large-ntp-py"' - this is the base CodeT5 model with next-token prediction, not the full CodeRL model with RL fine-tuning.

overall_assessment: The paper contains multiple significant hallucinations. Most critically: (1) The title and framing claim a comparison between RL and DPO alignment methods, but CodeLlama-Instruct is not DPO-aligned - it's a general instruction-tuned model. (2) At least two key citations appear to be fabricated (Wang et al. 2025 ICSE paper and the LlmFix framework). (3) The '19-category LlmFix taxonomy' is constructed by the authors but attributed to a non-existent paper. (4) The RL model used (codet5-large-ntp-py) is a base next-token prediction model, not the full CodeRL model with RL training. While the paper does acknowledge some confounds in its discussion, the fundamental mischaracterization of both models and the fabricated citations represent serious hallucinations.
